summary: Single-chain mapping for the GemPad locker benchmark. Although GemPad runs across multiple chains independently (BSC, ETH, others), the 2024-04 incident was BSC-only — source_chain == destination_chain. Cross-chain mapping fields are kept for schema parity but collapse to BSC.

destination_chain.name: bsc
destination_chain.note: Same contract — GemPad's BSC locker is the only contract relevant to the 2024-04 incident.

entity_map.role: GemPad token-locker on BSC. Holds project LP and team-allocation locks; the entire benchmark surface.
Project teams who locked liquidity / team tokens with GemPad. Each created at least one lock entry whose `owner` field the attacker hijacked.
Attacker EOA that called `transferLockOwnership(lockId, attacker)` followed by `withdraw(lockId)` for ~8 lock positions.

asset_map.notes: Representative PancakeSwap LP token locked by a project. Multiple LPs drained — the placeholder address stands in for the cluster.
Representative team-allocation token locked by a project.

bsc.vulnerable_param: newOwner — combined with missing auth check, anyone can hijack any lock's owner

message_map.critical_default: msg.sender unchecked against locks[lockId].owner in transferLockOwnership

invariants_for_fuzzer.description: transferLockOwnership(lockId, newOwner) must require msg.sender == locks[lockId].owner. The `withdraw` function's authority depends on this field being trustworthy.
Once a lock is created, its `owner` field may only change via an authorised path (signed by current owner OR via a time-locked governance step). Direct rewrites by arbitrary callers must revert.

A Lock may release at most `amount` tokens, exactly once, to the original depositor (Lock.owner at the time of `lock()`). Releasing to a hijacked owner violates the spirit of asset conservation per user even though the per-token sum is preserved.
withdraw must require block.timestamp >= unlockTime. (This invariant is honoured — it is not the bug surface.)
